name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ قَدْ جَآءَتْكُم مَّوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَشِفَآءٌ لِّمَا فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٥٧)
```

```english
O mankind! There has certainly come to you an advice from your Lord, and a cure for what is in the breasts, and a guidance and mercy for the faithful. (57)
```

```arabic
قُلْ بِفَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ وَبِرَحْمَتِهِۦ فَبِذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَفْرَحُوا۟ هُوَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ (٥٨)
```

```english
Say, ‘In Allah’s grace and His mercy—let them rejoice in that! It is better than what they amass.’ (58)
```

```arabic
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُم مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُم مِّن رِّزْقٍ فَجَعَلْتُم مِّنْهُ حَرَامًا وَحَلَـٰلًا قُلْ ءَآللَّهُ أَذِنَ لَكُمْ ۖ أَمْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَفْتَرُونَ (٥٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘Have you regarded what Allah has sent down for you of [His] provision, whereupon you have made some of it unlawful and [some] lawful?’ Say, ‘Did Allah give you the sanction [to do so], or do you fabricate a lie against Allah?’ (59)
```

```arabic
وَمَا ظَنُّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٦٠)
```

```english
What is the idea of those who fabricate lies against Allah [concerning their situation] on the Day of Resurrection? Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind, but most of them do not give thanks. (60)
```

```arabic
وَمَا تَكُونُ فِى شَأْنٍ وَمَا تَتْلُوا۟ مِنْهُ مِن قُرْءَانٍ وَلَا تَعْمَلُونَ مِنْ عَمَلٍ إِلَّا كُنَّا عَلَيْكُمْ شُهُودًا إِذْ تُفِيضُونَ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَا يَعْزُبُ عَن رَّبِّكَ مِن مِّثْقَالِ ذَرَّةٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَلَآ أَصْغَرَ مِن ذَٰلِكَ وَلَآ أَكْبَرَ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٦١)
```

```english
You do not engage in any work, neither do you recite any part of the Quran, nor do you perform any deed without Our being witness over you when you are engaged therein. Not an atom’s weight escapes your Lord in the earth or in the sky, nor [is there] anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book. (61)
```

```arabic
أَلَآ إِنَّ أَوْلِيَآءَ ٱللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦٢)
```

```english
Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve (62)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ (٦٣)
```

```english
—those who have faith and are Godwary. (63)
```

```arabic
لَهُمُ ٱلْبُشْرَىٰ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَفِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٦٤)
```

```english
For them is good news in the life of this world and in the Hereafter. (There is no altering the words of Allah.) That is the great success. (64)
```

```arabic
وَلَا يَحْزُنكَ قَوْلُهُمْ ۘ إِنَّ ٱلْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا ۚ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٦٥)
```

```english
Do not grieve at their remarks; indeed all might belongs to Allah; He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (65)
```

```arabic
أَلَآ إِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَمَا يَتَّبِعُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ ۚ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَإِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ (٦٦)
```

```english
Look! To Allah indeed belongs whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth. What do they pursue who invoke partners besides Allah? They merely follow conjectures and they just make surmises. (66)
```

```arabic
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَٱلنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَسْمَعُونَ (٦٧)
```

```english
It is He who made the night for you, that you may rest in it, and the day to provide visibility. There are indeed signs in that for people who listen. (67)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۖ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ۖ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنْ عِندَكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍۭ بِهَـٰذَآ ۚ أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٨)
```

```english
They say, ‘Allah has taken a son!’ Immaculate is He! He is the All-sufficient. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth. You have no authority for this [statement]. Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know? (68)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ لَا يُفْلِحُونَ (٦٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘Indeed those who fabricate lies against Allah will not be felicitous.’ (69)
```

```arabic
مَتَـٰعٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ثُمَّ إِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ نُذِيقُهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلشَّدِيدَ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ (٧٠)
```

```english
An enjoyment in this world; then to Us shall be their return, then We shall make them taste the severe punishment because of what they used to defy. (70)
```

## (Commentary)

The discourse in these verses returns to describing the Noble Quran with its noble attributes, followed by miscellaneous points connected to the previous discourse regarding the purpose of the Surah. Within them are exhortation, wisdom, and proof for various objectives, as well as a description of the friends of Allah (أولياء الله) and glad tidings for them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ قَدْ جَاءَتْكُم مَّوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ» — **O mankind! There has certainly come to you an advice from your Lord** to the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "Exhortation (الوعظ) is a deterrent coupled with intimidation." Al-Khalil said: "It is a reminder of good in a way that softens the heart." *Al-Idhah* (العظة) and *al-Maw'izah* (الموعظة) are the nouns. (End of quote). The chest (الصدر) is well-known; since people found the heart within the chest, and they perceived that a human only perceives what he perceives through his heart—by which he understands matters, loves, hates, intends, dislikes, yearns, hopes, and wishes—they considered the chest to be the treasury for the secrets of the heart and the spiritual attributes (الصفات الروحية) within the human interior, including virtues and vices. Within virtues lie the health and uprightness of the heart, and within vices lie its sickness and disease. Vice is a malady (داء); it is said: "I healed my chest with such-and-such" when that which was in the chest of constriction and distress has departed. It is also said: "I healed my heart." Thus, the healing of the chests (شفاء الصدور) and the healing of what is in the chests is an allusion to the departure of the base spiritual attributes that bring misery to the human, disturb his happy life, and deprive him of the good of this world and the Hereafter.

Guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — is the pointing toward the sought objective with gentleness, as mentioned by al-Raghib. A discussion on this has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: «فَمَن يُرِدِ اللَّهُ أَن يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلَامِ» — **Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam** (Al-An'am: 125) in the seventh volume of this book.

Mercy (الرحمة) is a specific affection in the heart arising from witnessing harm or deficiency in another, which impels the merciful one to mend the fracture and complete the deficiency. When attributed to Him, the Exalted, it carries the meaning of the result (النتيجة) rather than the origin of the affection, due to His transcendence above such things. Thus, it applies to His absolute bestowal and His emanation of existence (إفاضة الوجود) upon His creation.

His bestowal, when attributed to His creation in general, refers to what is attributed to Him of their existence, survival, and the provision (الرزق) by which their survival is sustained, along with all other blessings He bestows upon them, which are too numerous to count: «وَإِن تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ لَا تُحْصُوهَا» — **and if you enumerate Allah’s blessing, you will not be able to count it**. When attributed specifically to the believers (المؤمنين), it refers to what is exclusive to them of the happiness of human life in its various manifestations—the blessings Allah bestows upon them of true divine gnosis (المعارف الحقة الإلهية), noble character, righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة), the good life in this world and the Hereafter, Paradise, and Divine Pleasure (الرضوان).

Hence, when the Quran is described as a mercy for the believers, it means that it envelops the believers with types of goodness and blessings that Allah has treasured within it for those who realize its realities and clothe themselves in its meanings. He, the Exalted, said: «وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ مَا هُوَ شِفَاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۙ وَلَا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَسَارًا» — **We send down in the Quran that which is a healing and mercy for the faithful, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss** (Al-Isra: 82).

If these four attributes that Allah, the Glorified, enumerated for the Quran in this verse—namely that it is an advice (موعظة), a healing for what is in the breasts (شفاء لما في الصدور), a guidance (هدى), and a mercy (رحمة)—are taken and compared to one another, and then considered in relation to the Quran, the verse becomes a comprehensive explanation of its general beautiful effect and its pure, sacred knowledge that it inscribes in the souls of the believers, from the first moment it strikes their ears to the final point where it takes hold of their souls and settles in their hearts.

For it reaches them first when they are enveloped by the sea of heedlessness and surrounded by the abyss of confusion, such that their interior is darkened by the shadows of doubt and suspicion, and their hearts are sickened by the maladies of vices and every base or wicked state. It then exhorts them with a beautiful exhortation, awakening them from the slumber of heedlessness, deterring them from their evil inner states and wicked deeds, and impelling them toward goodness and happiness.

Then it begins to purify their inner selves from base attributes, continuously removing the afflictions of the intellects and the diseases of the hearts, one after another, until it reaches the last of them.

Then it guides them to true gnosis, noble character, and righteous deeds—a guidance with gentleness, raising them degree by degree and bringing them closer station by station, until they settle in the station of the near ones (المقربين) and attain the triumph of the sincere (المخلصين).

Then it clothes them in the garment of mercy, settles them in the abode of honor, and seats them upon the throne of happiness, until it joins them with the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous—and excellent are these as companions—and enters them into the company of His near servants in the highest of the high (أعلى عليين).

Thus, the Quran is an exhorter, a healer for what is in the breasts, a guide to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), and an emanator of mercy by the permission of Allah, the Glorified. It only exhorts through what is within it, heals the breasts, guides, and spreads mercy by itself, not by any other matter, for it is the connected means between Allah and His creation. Thus, it is an advice, a healing for what is in the breasts, a guidance, and a mercy for the believers. Understand this.

The Glorified opened the verse with His saying: «يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ» — **O mankind!**, which is an address to people in general, not just the polytheists or the polytheists of Mecca specifically, even if the verse occurs in the context of discourse with them. This is because the attributes mentioned therein—«قَدْ جَاءَتْكُم مَّوْعِظَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَشِفَاءٌ لِّمَا فِي الصُّدُورِ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ» — **there has certainly come to you an advice from your Lord, and a healing for what is in the breasts, and a guidance and a mercy for the faithful**—pertain to all of them, not to a specific group among them.

Among the strange interpretations is the claim of some that "mercy" refers to the mercy and compassion that believers show toward one another; this is an error that the context absolutely refutes.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قُلْ بِفَضْلِ اللَّهِ وَبِرَحْمَتِهِ فَبِذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَفْرَحُوا هُوَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ» — **Say, ‘In Allah’s grace and His mercy—let them rejoice in that! It is better than what they amass.’** **Grace** (الفضل) is an increase or surplus. A gift is called *fadl* because the giver usually gives what he does not need of wealth. In naming what Allah emanates upon His servants as *fadl*, there is an indication of His self-sufficiency (الغنى) and His lack of need in His emanation for what He emanates or for the one upon whom He emanates.

It is not far-fetched that "grace" refers to the bounty Allah spreads over all His creation, and "mercy" refers specifically to what He emanates upon the believers. For when the mercy of religious happiness is joined with the general blessing of life, provision, and other general blessings, the combination of the two is more deserving of joy and delight, and more worthy of expansion and jubilation.

This may be supported by His saying: «بِفَضْلِ اللَّهِ وَبِرَحْمَتِهِ» — **In Allah’s grace and His mercy**, where the causal *ba* (باء السببية) is introduced before both "grace" and "mercy." This suggests that each of them is an independent cause, even though they are joined a second time by His saying: «فَبِذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَفْرَحُوا» — **let them rejoice in that**, to indicate that their combination is worthy of being the sole object of joy.

It is also possible that "grace" refers to matters other than "mercy" mentioned in the previous verse—namely, the advice, the healing for what is in the breast, and the guidance—while "mercy" refers to mercy in the sense mentioned in the previous verse, which is the specific divine gift that constitutes the happiness of life in this world and the Hereafter.

The meaning according to this is that what Allah has graced them with of advice, healing for what is in the breasts, and guidance, and what He has shown mercy to the believers with of the good life—that is more worthy for them to rejoice in than the wealth they amass.

This view may be supported by His saying, the Glorified: «وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ مَا زَكَىٰ مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَدًا وَلَٰكِنَّ اللَّهَ يُزَكِّي مَن يَشَاءُ» — **Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever be pure, but Allah purifies whomever He wishes** (Al-Nur: 21), where He attributed their purification to both grace and mercy together. Attributing purification to "grace" in the sense of a general gift is far from the understanding. What further supports this view is its harmony with what has been narrated in reports interpreting the verse as referring to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and Ali (عليه السلام), or to the Quran and being specialized with it, as will come, Allah willing.

Regarding His saying: «فَبِذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَفْرَحُوا» — **let them rejoice in that**, they mentioned that the *fa* in «فَلْيَفْرَحُوا» is extra (زائدة), as in the poet's saying: "If I am killed, then at that moment, grieve (فاجزعي)." The adverbial phrase «فَبِذَٰلِكَ» is a substitute for «بِفَضْلِ اللَّهِ وَبِرَحْمَتِهِ» and is connected to «فَلْيَفْرَحُوا», placed before it to signify restriction (الحصر). His saying: «هُوَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ» — **It is better than what they amass** is a second explanation of the meaning of restriction.

From all this, it becomes clear that the verse is a derivation from the content of the previous verse. When He addressed mankind as a favor to them, stating that this Quran is an advice for them, a healing for what is in their breasts, a guidance, and a mercy for the believers among them, He derived from this that they ought to rejoice in this grace and mercy He favored them with, not in the wealth they amass. For this—which contains their happiness and that upon which their happiness depends—is better than wealth, which is nothing but a trial (فتنة) that might destroy them and make them miserable.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ مَا أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ مِنْ رِزْقٍ فَجَعَلْتُمْ مِنْهُ حَرَامًا وَحَلَالًا» — **Say, ‘Have you regarded the provision that Allah has sent down for you, whereat you have made some of it unlawful and [some] lawful?’** to the end of the verse. Attributing provision (الرزق)—which is what sustains a human in his survival of earthly matters like food, drink, clothing, and others—to "sending down" (الإنزال) is based on a reality provided by the Quran: that things have treasuries with Allah from which they descend according to what Allah, the Glorified, has decreed. He, the Exalted, said: «وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ» — **There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except by a known measure** (Al-Hijr: 21). He also said: «وَفِي السَّمَاءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَمَا تُوعَدُونَ» — **And in the sky is your provision and what you are promised** (Al-Dhariyat: 22). He said: «وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ الْأَنْعَامِ ثَمَانِيَةَ أَزْوَاجٍ» — **and He has sent down for you eight mates of the cattle** (Al-Zumar: 6). And He said: «وَأَنزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ» — **And We sent down iron** (Al-Hadid: 25).

As for what has been said—that the expression "sending down" is used because the provisions of the servants come from the rain that Allah sends down from the sky—this is a simplistic view that does not hold true, even if correct, in all instances where their existence is expressed as "sending down," such as with cattle and iron. The provision that the verse mentions Allah sent down for them, of which they made some unlawful and some lawful, refers to cattle such as camels and sheep, like the *wasilah*, *sa'ibah*, *ham*, and others.

The *lam* in His saying: «لَكُمْ» — **for you** is for purpose (للغاية) and conveys the meaning of benefit; i.e., Allah sent it down for your sake and for you to benefit from it. It is not for transitivity (للتعدية), as "sending down" is made transitive with *'ala* (upon) or *ila* (to). From here, the speech conveys the meaning of permissibility and lawfulness; i.e., Allah sent them down and thus made them lawful. This is the subtle point in mentioning prohibition before lawfulness in His saying: «فَجَعَلْتُمْ مِنْهُ حَرَامًا وَحَلَالًا» — **whereat you have made some of it unlawful and [some] lawful**; meaning, Allah had made it lawful for you by sending it down as provision for you to benefit from in your life and survival, but you divided it into two parts of your own accord, prohibiting one part and allowing another. The meaning is: Say to them, O Muhammad: Inform me about the lawful provision Allah sent down for you and for your sake, which you then divided into two parts, making some of it unlawful and some lawful—what is the reason for that? It is clear that this is a fabrication against Allah, not by permission from Him, the Exalted.

His saying: «قُلْ آللَّهُ أَذِنَ لَكُمْ ۖ أَمْ عَلَى اللَّهِ تَفْتَرُونَ» — **Say, ‘Did Allah give you leave, or do you fabricate lies against Allah?’** is a question about the reason for their division of provision into unlawful and lawful. Since it is clear that this was not by permission from Him, the Exalted—due to their lack of connection to their Lord through revelation or a messenger—it is certain that it is a fabrication. Thus, the interrogation in the context of an alternative (الترديد) is an allusion to affirming the fabrication against them, and a rebuke and censure.

What initial reflection dictates is that the alternative in the verse is not exhaustive. Just as it is possible for their division of Allah's provision into unlawful and lawful to be by permission from Allah or a fabrication against Him, it is also possible for it to be based on a benefit they secured or imagined, or due to a whim they had, without attributing it to Allah, in which case it would not be a fabrication against Him.

From another perspective, the alternative in the verse between Allah's permission and fabrication against Allah suggests that judgment (الحكم) belongs only to Allah. Thus, the judgment that some provision is unlawful and some is lawful—which circulates among them—must either be from Allah or a fabrication against Him. It is possible to deny this at first glance, as many norms circulating among people were formed by the nature of their society or their national customs and so on.

However, contemplation of His speech, the Exalted, and deep research refutes this. The Quran holds that judgment belongs exclusively to Allah, and no one among His creation has the right to initiate the legislation of a rule and place it within human society. He, the Exalted, said: «إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ» — **Sovereignty belongs only to Allah** (Yusuf: 40).

He, the Exalted, pointed to the reason for this in His saying: «فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا ۚ فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ» — **So set your face on the religion as a Hanif, with the nature of Allah which He has created in mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion** (Al-Rum: 30). It is thus clarified that the meaning of judgment belonging to Allah is its reliance upon creation and the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — being in conformity with it and not contradicting what existence and being proclaim.

This is because Allah, the Glorified, did not create the creation in vain, as He said: «أَفَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّمَا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ عَبَثًا» — **Did you suppose that We created you aimlessly?** (Al-Mu'minun: 115). Rather, He created them for divine purposes and perfect goals toward which they turn according to their constitution and toward which they proceed by their nature, using the means and tools He equipped them with, and the path He made easy for them, as He said: «أَعْطَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدَىٰ» — **He gave everything its creation and then guided it** (Taha: 50), and He said: «ثُمَّ السَّبِيلَ يَسَّرَهُ» — **then He made the way easy for him** (Abasa: 20).

Thus, the existence of things at the start of their creation is suited to the station of perfection prepared for them, equipped with powers and tools to reach their goal. None of them proceeds toward its prepared perfection except through the path of acquired attributes and actions. In view of this, it is necessary that the religion (الدين)—meaning the laws governing acquired attributes and actions—be in conformity with creation and nature. For nature does not forget its goal nor overstep it, and it does not impel toward an act or deter from an act except due to the call of what it was equipped with, and the equipment only calls for that for which it was equipped, which is the goal.

Since the human is equipped with the apparatus for nutrition and marriage, his true judgment in the religion of nature is nutrition and marriage, not asceticism or monasticism, for example. Since he is naturally disposed toward society and cooperation, it is part of his judgment to participate with other people in their society and perform social acts, and so on.

Therefore, the judgments and norms determined for the human are those to which the universal existence (الكون العالمي)—of which he is a small part—calls him, and his existence has been equipped with what drives him toward the stage of perfection. This general universe, whose parts are interconnected and which is the manifestation of Allah's will, is the bearer of the human primordial law (الشريعة الفطرية) and the caller to the upright religion of Allah.

Thus, the true religion is the judgment of Allah, the Glorified; there is no judgment but His, and it is that which conforms to the divine creation. Any judgment beyond it is falsehood that only leads the human to misery and destruction and only guides him to the punishment of the Blaze.

From here, the two previous knots are untied. Since judgment belongs to Allah, the Glorified, alone, every judgment circulating among people is either a judgment of Allah in reality, taken from Him through revelation or messengership, or a judgment fabricated against Allah; there is no third category.

Furthermore, the polytheists used to attribute such judgments they innovated and established among themselves to Allah, the Glorified, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: «وَإِذَا فَعَلُوا فَاحِشَةً قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهَا آبَاءَنَا وَاللَّهُ أَمَرَنَا بِهَا» — **When they commit an indecency, they say, ‘We found our fathers practicing it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us.’** (Al-A'raf: 28).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَمَا ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ» — **What is the idea of those who fabricate lies against Allah about the Day of Resurrection?** to the end of the verse. Since the answer to the previous interrogation—«آللَّهُ أَذِنَ لَكُمْ ۖ أَمْ عَلَى اللَّهِ تَفْتَرُونَ» — **Did Allah give you leave, or do you fabricate lies against Allah?**—was known from the context, namely that it is fabrication, He magnified the gravity of its consequence. For it is a fabrication against Allah, the Glorified, and fabrication is among the sins and offenses by self-evident judgment, so it inevitably has an evil effect. Therefore, He, the Exalted, said as a threat and warning: «وَمَا ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ».

As for His saying: «إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ» — **Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind, but most of them do not give thanks**, it is a complaint and reproach referring to what people have become accustomed to—the ingratitude of most of them for Allah's blessing and their lack of gratitude in the face of His gift and blessing. What is meant by "grace" (الفضل) here is the divine gift, for the discourse is about the provision Allah sent down for them, which is the grace, and their prohibition of some of it, which is ingratitude and lack of thanks.

By linking the end of the verse to its beginning, fabrication against Allah becomes one of the instances of ingratitude for His blessing. The meaning is that Allah is the possessor of grace and bestowal upon mankind, but most of them are ungrateful for His blessing and grace; so what is the thought of those who are ungrateful for Allah's blessing and provision by prohibiting it as a fabrication of lies against Allah on the Day of Resurrection?

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَمَا تَكُونُ فِي شَأْنٍ وَمَا تَتْلُو مِنْهُ مِنْ قُرْآنٍ وَلَا تَعْمَلُونَ مِنْ عَمَلٍ إِلَّا كُنَّا عَلَيْكُمْ شُهُودًا» — **You do not engage in any matter, nor do you recite any part of the Quran, nor do you perform any deed without Our being witness over you** to the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: **The matter** (الشأن) is the state and the affair that occurs and is suitable; it is only said regarding great states and affairs. He said: «كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ» — **Every day He is engaged in some work**. (End of quote).

His saying: «وَمَا تَتْلُو مِنْهُ مِنْ قُرْآنٍ» — **nor do you recite any part of the Quran**, it appears that the pronoun refers to Allah, the Glorified. The first *min* is for origin and emergence, and the second is for explanation. The meaning is: you do not recite anything that is the Quran emerging and descending from His side, the Glorified. "Engaging" (الإفاضة) in an act is to immerse oneself in it collectively.

In His saying: «إِلَّا كُنَّا عَلَيْكُمْ شُهُودًا» — **without Our being witness over you**, a shift (التفات) has occurred from the third person to the first person plural. The subtle point in this is to indicate the multiplicity of witnesses, for Allah has witnesses over the deeds of people among the angels and humans, and Allah is encompassing from behind them. Great ones speak on behalf of themselves and others to indicate that they have helpers and servants.

One should not overlook that the origin of the shift begins from the start of the verse. The previous verses were addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and taking the polytheists in the third person, speaking to them through him without facing him with any address specific to himself. This verse has turned the direction of speech to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding what concerns him specifically, saying: «وَمَا تَكُونُ فِي شَأْنٍ وَمَا تَتْلُو مِنْهُ مِنْ قُرْآنٍ» — **You do not engage in any matter, nor do you recite any part of the Quran**. Then it joined him, the polytheists, and all others in a single address, saying: «وَلَا تَعْمَلُونَ مِنْ عَمَلٍ إِلَّا كُنَّا عَلَيْكُمْ شُهُودًا» — **nor do you perform any deed without Our being witness over you**. This was done by joining them to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while they were in the third person, and spreading the address over everyone by way of predominance (التغليب), as when you say to your interlocutor: "You and your people do such and such."

The evidence that this address is by way of joining and predominance is His saying afterward: «وَمَا يَعْزُبُ عَنْ رَبِّكَ» — **and not [as much as] the weight of an atom eludes your Lord**, for it reveals that the address with him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) continues as it was.

In any case, the mentioned turn in the address of the verse is to indicate that the divine sovereignty and complete encompassment apply to deeds as witnessing and knowledge in the most perfect manner from every side, without excluding any prophet, believer, or polytheist, and without overlooking any deed. Let no one imagine that anything of his affair is hidden from Allah such that He will not call him to account for it on the Day of Resurrection; let this be his thought of his Lord on the Day of Resurrection, and let him take heed.

The mention of reciting the Quran independently, despite its inclusion in His previous saying: «وَمَا تَكُونُ فِي شَأْنٍ» — **You do not engage in any matter**—as it is one of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) matters—is to signal the importance of its affair and increased concern for it.

In the verse, there is first a strengthening of the exhortation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his nation. Second: that which the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recites of the Quran to the people from Allah's revelation and His speech is not touched by change, nor does falsehood creep into it, neither in its reception from Allah nor in its recitation to the people. Thus, the verse is close in content to His saying: «عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلَا يُظْهِرُ عَلَىٰ غَيْبِهِ أَحَدًا إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ مِن رَّسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَدًا لِّيَعْلَمَ أَن قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسَالَاتِ رَبِّهِمْ» — **Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before him and behind him, so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord** (Al-Jinn: 26-28).

And His saying: «وَمَا يَعْزُبُ عَنْ رَبِّكَ مِنْ مِثْقَالِ ذَرَّةٍ» — **and not [as much as] the weight of an atom eludes your Lord** to the end of the verse. Eluding (العزوب) is absence, distance, and hiddenness. In it is an indication of the presence of things with Him, the Exalted, without absence, and His preservation of them in a book without passing away. Some of what pertains to this has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: «وَعِندَهُ مَفَاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ» — **With Him are the treasures of the Unseen** (Al-An'am: 59) in the seventh volume of this book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «أَلَا إِنَّ أَوْلِيَاءَ اللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ» — **Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve** is a resumption of the discourse, yet it is connected to the purpose of the Surah, which is the call to faith in the Book of Allah and the invitation to the monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — in its broad sense.

To indicate the importance of the matter, it opened with the particle of alerting «أَلَا» (Look!). Allah, the Glorified, mentions in this verse and the two verses after it His friends, defining them, describing the effects of their guardianship (ولاية), and the specific characteristics they possess.

Guardianship (الولاية), although many meanings have been mentioned for it, its linguistic root is the removal of the intervening medium between two things such that there is nothing between them that is not of them. Then it was used metaphorically for the proximity of one thing to another in any form of proximity, such as proximity in lineage, place, station, friendship, or otherwise. Therefore, the term "guardian" (الولي) is applied to both parties of the guardianship, especially considering that each of them manages (يلي) from the other what no one else manages. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the guardian of His believing servant because He manages his affair and disposes of his state, guiding him to His straight path, commanding and forbidding him in what is appropriate or inappropriate for him, and helping him in the life of this world and in the Hereafter.

The true believer is the guardian of his Lord because he manages from Him obedience to His command and prohibition, and he manages from Him all spiritual blessings of guidance, success, support, and strengthening, and the subsequent honoring with Paradise and Divine Pleasure.

The friends of Allah (أولياء الله), in any case, are the believers, for Allah considers Himself their guardian in their spiritual life where He says: «وَاللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» — **and Allah is the guardian of the faithful** (Al-Imran: 68).

However, the verse following this one, which interprets the word, refuses to allow guardianship to include all believers, among whom are those about whom Allah, the Glorified, says: «وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ» — **And most of them do not believe in Allah without being polytheists** (Yusuf: 106). For His saying in the following verse: «الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكَانُوا يَتَّقُونَ» — **those who have faith and are God-conscious** defines them by faith and godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being God-conscious and protecting oneself from His displeasure* — while indicating that they were in a state of continuous godwariness prior to their faith in terms of time, as it says: «آمَنُوا» (they believed) and then says, conjoined to it: «وَكَانُوا يَتَّقُونَ» (and they used to be God-conscious). This indicates that they were continuing in godwariness before the realization of this faith from them. It is well-known that initial faith is not preceded by godwariness; rather, they are concurrent, or faith is before godwariness, especially continuous godwariness.

Thus, what is meant by this faith is another level among the levels of faith (مراتب الإيمان) other than the first level. It has already preceded in the first volume of this book, under verse 130 of al-Baqarah, that each of faith and submission (الإسلام), as well as polytheism (الشرك) and disbelief (الكفر), has different levels, some above others. The first level of Islam is the utterance of the two testimonies with the tongue and outward submission. This is followed by the first level of faith, which is the internal conviction (الإذعان) of the content of the two testimonies in the heart generally, even if it has not permeated all that is believed in the religion of true belief. Therefore, it is possible for it to coexist with polytheism from some aspects. He, the Exalted, said: «وَمَا يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُم بِاللَّهِ إِلَّا وَهُم مُّشْرِكُونَ» — **And most of them do not believe in Allah without being polytheists** (Yusuf: 106).

The submission of the servant continues to purify and grow until his submission to Allah, the Glorified, encompasses everything that returns to Him—and to Him is the return of every matter. Whenever Islam rises a degree and ascends a level, the faith appropriate to it is the conviction in the requirements of that level, until the servant submits to his Lord in the true sense of His divinity (ألوهيته), and resentment and objection cease within him. He does not resent any of His affairs of decree, destiny, or judgment, nor does he object to any of His will. Corresponding to that is faith with certainty (اليقين) in Allah and all that pertains to Him, which is the complete faith by which the servant's servitude (عبودية) is perfected.

He, the Exalted, said: «فَلَا وَرَبِّكَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ حَتَّىٰ يُحَكِّمُوكَ فِيمَا شَجَرَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُوا فِي أَنفُسِهِمْ حَرَجًا مِّمَّا قَضَيْتَ وَيُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيمًا» — **But no, by your Lord! They will not believe until they make you a judge in their disputes, then do not find within their souls any burning regret over your verdict and submit in total submission** (Al-Nisa: 65). It is most likely that this level of faith, or what is close to it, is what is meant by the verse: «الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكَانُوا يَتَّقُونَ» — **those who have faith and are God-conscious**, for it is the faith preceded by continuous godwariness, not faith in its first level, as mentioned.

Furthermore, His describing the people of this faith as those who «لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ» — **will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve** indicates that what is meant is the high degree of faith with which the meaning of servitude and pure ownership (المملوكية المحضة) for the servant is completed. In this state, he sees that sovereignty belongs to Allah alone without partner, and that he has no part in the matter such that he would fear its loss or grieve for its absence.

This is because fear (الخوف) only occurs to the soul from the expectation of harm returning to it, and grief (الحزن) only happens to it for the loss of what it loves or the realization of what it dislikes of that whose benefit or harm returns to it. This cannot be realized except in what a person sees as his own possession or a right connected to what he fears for or grieves for losing, such as children, wealth, status, or otherwise. As for that with which a person has no connection whatsoever, he does not fear for it nor grieve for its loss at all.

He who sees everything as the absolute property of Allah, the Glorified, with no one sharing in His kingdom, does not see for himself any ownership or right regarding anything such that he would fear in its regard or grieve. This is the one whom Allah describes as being among His friends when He says: «أَلَا إِنَّ أَوْلِيَاءَ اللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ» — **Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve**. These people fear nothing and grieve for nothing, neither in this world nor in the Hereafter, except as Allah wills—and He has willed that they fear their Lord and grieve for what they might miss of His honor, if they miss it. All of this is part of submission to Allah; so understand that.

The absolute nature (إطلاق) of the verse implies their being characterized by these two descriptions—the absence of fear and the absence of grief—in both realms, this world and the Hereafter. As for verses like His saying, the Exalted: «إِلَّا الْمُتَّقِينَ يَا عِبَادِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمُ الْيَوْمَ وَلَا أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَكَانُوا مُسْلِمِينَ» — **except the God-conscious. ‘O My servants! Today you will have no fear, nor will you grieve—those who believed in Our signs and had submitted’** (Al-Zukhruf: 67-69), although the apparent meaning of the verses is that they refer to the friends in the sense described in the verse we are discussing, the affirmation of the absence of fear and grief for them on the Day of Resurrection does not negate it from them at other times. Yes, there is a difference from another aspect, which is the purity of the blessing and honor and the reaching of its clarity on the Day of Resurrection, whereas it is mixed and not pure at other times.

Similar to it is His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقَامُوا تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ أَلَّا تَخَافُوا وَلَا تَحْزَنُوا وَأَبْشِرُوا بِالْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ نَحْنُ أَوْلِيَاؤُكُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ» — **Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah!’ and then remain steadfast, the angels descend upon them [saying]: ‘Do not fear, nor be grieved! Receive the good news of the paradise, which you have been promised. We are your friends in the life of this world and in the Hereafter’** (Fussilat: 30-31). Although the verses appear to indicate that this descent, speech, and glad tidings occur at the time of death because of His saying: «كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ» — **you have been promised** and His saying: «أَبْشِرُوا» — **Receive the good news**, the affirmation at one time is not sufficient for negation at another time, as you have known.

This is what the verse indicates according to the absolute nature of its wording and the support of other verses for it. Most commentators have restricted His saying: «لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ» — **will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve**—relying on the verses of the Hereafter—to the day of death and the Resurrection. They neglected what the specific wording provides in His saying: «الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكَانُوا يَتَّقُونَ» — **those who have faith and are God-conscious**, and they took faith and godwariness as two concurrent matters. Thus, the meaning returned to: the friends of Allah are the God-conscious among the people of faith, and there is no fear for them in the Hereafter nor will they grieve. This—as you have known—is a restriction without a restrictor.

Some of them generalized the negation of fear and grief, mentioning that they are characterized by it in this world and the Hereafter, but he corrupted the meaning from another side, saying: The "friends" as interpreted by the second verse are all the God-conscious among the believers, and the meaning of their lack of fear and grief is that they do not fear in the Hereafter what the disbelievers, the transgressors, and the wrongdoers fear of the terrors of the station and the punishment of the Hereafter, nor do they grieve over what they left behind; and that they do not fear in this world as the disbelievers fear, nor do they grieve as they grieve.

He said: "As for the origin of fear and grief, it is among the human accidents (الأعراض البشرية) from which no one is safe in this world. Rather, the righteous believers are the most patient of people and the most satisfied with Allah's norms, believing and knowing that if He tries them with something that causes fear or grief, He only nurtures them thereby to perfect their souls and refine them through struggle in His way, by which their reward increases, as many verses have stated." (End of quote).

As for his restricting the verse such that what is negated from the friends is the fear and grief that occur to the disbelievers, excluding what occurs to the general believers according to human nature, and his reliance on many verses for this—it is a restriction without a restrictor. As for his saying that the origin of fear and grief is something from which no one is safe at all, it stems from a failure to grasp the intended meaning of the speech due to a lack of depth in researching high ethics and human spiritual stations (المقامات المعنوية). This led him to measure the state of the honored among Allah's near servants—the prophets and the friends—against what he finds of the state of the average among the general people. He thus imagined that the accidents that envelop the general public, which he called natural states, must inevitably envelop the elite, and that what is impossible or difficult for the average is likewise for the perfect. In that case, nothing remains for the spiritual stations and real degrees except that they are names behind which there is no reality, and conventional considerations (اعتبارات وضعية) agreed upon similar to the illusory stations and official social degrees we circulate in our societies for the benefit of social order.

Thus, he neither fulfilled the right of scholarly research such that it would guide him to the true result—clarifying that complete monotheism (التوحيد الكامل) restricts the reality of sovereignty to Allah, the Glorified, such that no independence in influence remains for any other, whereby love, hate, fear, grief, joy, sorrow, or anything else would be attached to it for its own sake. Rather, this person who is enveloped by monotheism only fears, grieves, loves, or hates through Allah, the Glorified. The contradiction then vanishes between our saying: "he fears nothing but Allah" and our saying: "he fears much of what harms him and guards against matters he dislikes." Understand that.

Nor did he master the Quranic research and exhaust his effort in it such that it would become clear to him that His saying, the Exalted: «أَلَا إِنَّ أَوْلِيَاءَ اللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ» — **Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve**—negated fear and grief absolutely without restriction to any thing or state, except what verses have stated regarding the obligation of fearing Allah. Thus, these people do not fear anything in this world or the Hereafter except Allah, the Glorified, nor do they grieve.

As for the many verses that describe the believers as having no fear or grief at death or on the Day of Resurrection, they only describe their states in a specific circumstance, and the negation or affirmation of something in one instance does not necessitate its opposite in another, which is obvious.

The verse, moreover, indicates that this description belongs to a specific group of believers who are distinguished from others by a specific level of faith that belongs to them and not to the general believers. This is according to what interprets it in His saying: «الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكَانُوا يَتَّقُونَ» — **those who have faith and are God-conscious** as per the previously established evidence.

In short, the removal of fear of other than Allah and grief from the friends does not mean that good and evil, benefit and harm, salvation and destruction, ease and hardship, pleasure and pain, and blessing and trial are equal to them and similar in their perception; for the human intellect—indeed, the general animal sensation—does not accept that.

Rather, it means that they do not see any independence in influence for other than Him, the Exalted, at all. They restrict sovereignty and judgment to Him, the Exalted, so they fear none but Him, or what Allah loves and wants them to guard against or grieve over.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «لَهُمُ الْبُشْرَىٰ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِكَلِمَاتِ اللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ» — **For them is good news in the life of this world and in the Hereafter. There is no altering the words of Allah. That is the great triumph.** Allah, the Exalted, gives them a general glad tiding of that which will delight their eyes. If His saying: «لَهُمُ الْبُشْرَىٰ» — **For them is good news** is an initiation of the glad tiding, its meaning is the occurrence of what was promised in both the world and the Hereafter. If it is an announcement that Allah will give them glad tidings, then the glad tiding occurs in the world and the Hereafter. As for the object of the glad tiding, does it occur in the Hereafter only or in both the world and the Hereafter? The verse is silent on that.

Glad tidings have occurred in His speech, the Exalted, for the believers that apply to the friends of Allah, such as His saying, the Exalted: «وَكَانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنَا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» — **and it was a must for Us to help the faithful** (Al-Rum: 47), and His saying: «إِنَّا لَنَنصُرُ رُسُلَنَا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهَادُ» — **We shall certainly help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of this world and on the day when the witnesses rise up** (Al-Mu'min: 51), and His saying: «بُشْرَاكُمُ الْيَوْمَ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ» — **‘The good news for you today is gardens with streams running in them’** (Al-Hadid: 12), among others.

His saying: «لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِكَلِمَاتِ اللَّهِ» — **There is no altering the words of Allah** is an indication that this is of the decisive decree (القضاء المحتوم) that has no way to change, and in it is a comforting of their souls.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَلَا يَحْزُنكَ قَوْلُهُمْ ۘ إِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا ۚ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ» — **Do not let their remarks grieve you; indeed all might belongs to Allah; He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing.** This is a refinement for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by consoling and comforting him regarding what they used to harm him with—attacking his Lord, slandering his religion, and priding themselves on their partners and deities, as suggested by the discourse in the following verse. He was almost grieved for Allah's sake, so Allah comforted him and soothed his soul by reminding him of what would calm his distress: that might (العزة) belongs to Allah, and that He is hearing of their speech, knowing of his state and their state. Since all might belongs to Him, the Exalted, no weight is given to the illusory might they pride themselves on, so let them rave as they will. Since He is All-hearing and All-knowing, if He willed, He would seize them with exemplary punishment; and since He does not seize them, it is only for the benefit of the mission and the goodness of the outcome.

From here it becomes clear that both His saying: «إِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ» — **indeed all might belongs to Allah** and His saying: «هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ» — **He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing** are independent reasons for the prohibition; hence they were brought separately without conjunction.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «أَلَا إِنَّ لِلَّهِ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَن فِي الْأَرْضِ» — **Look! To Allah indeed belongs whoever is in the heavens and whoever is in the earth** to the end of the verse. In it is an explanation of His ownership (مالكيته), the Exalted, of whoever is in the heavens and the earth, by which the meaning of Lordship (الربوبية) is completed for the Deity. For the Lord (الرب) is the owner who disposes of the affair of His possession. This ownership belongs to Allah alone without partner. Thus, the partners they call upon have no share in the meaning of partnership except what exists in the imagination of the callers and in their conjecture (خرصهم)—a concept that has no referent.

The verse compares their partners to Him, the Exalted, and judges that their relation to Him, the Exalted, is the relation of conjecture and guesswork to reality and truth. The rest is clear.

It was said: «مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَن فِي الْأَرْضِ» — **whoever is in the heavens and whoever is in the earth** and it did not say "whatever" (ما) because the discourse is about the Lordship of the servants who possess consciousness and intellect, namely the angels, humans, and jinn.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ لِتَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ وَالنَّهارَ مُبْصِراً» — **It is He who made the night for you that you may rest in it, and the day to provide light.** The verse completes the explanation provided in the previous verse to establish His Lordship, the Exalted. Lordship—as you know—is ownership and disposal (التدبير). His ownership, the Exalted, was mentioned in the previous verse; so by mentioning one of His general disposals in this verse, by which the general livelihood of people is set right and their lives are sustained, the meaning of Lordship is completed for Him.

To indicate this disposal, He mentioned with the night their rest in it, and with the day their seeing in it, which impels them to various movements and travels to acquire the materials of life and set right the affairs of livelihood. The matter of human life is not completed by movement alone or by rest alone. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, disposed the matter with the darkness of the night, which calls for the renewal of the equipment of powers after the exhaustion, fatigue, and toil that afflicted them, and for relaxation, intimacy with family, enjoyment of what was gathered and earned during the day, and leisure for servitude; and with the light of the day, which calls for sight, then yearning, then seeking.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَانَهُ ۖ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ ۖ لَهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ» — **They say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! He is the All-sufficient. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth.** Procreation (الاستيلاد) in its well-known sense among people is for a living being to separate some parts of its matter and nurture it through pregnancy or eggs in a gradual nurturing until it becomes an individual like itself. Humans, in particular, may seek a child to be a helper against the calamities of time and a treasure for the day of poverty. This meaning, in all its aspects, is impossible for Him, the Exalted. He—exalted is His Name—is transcendent above parts, exalted above gradualness in His act, free from likeness and resemblance, and self-sufficient (مستغن) from other than Himself by His essence.

The Quran has negated the child from Him by arguing against it from each of the mentioned aspects, as it addressed its negation from all of them in His saying: «وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَانَهُ ۖ بَل لَّهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُ قَانِتُونَ بَدِيعُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ» — **And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He! Rather, to Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. All are obedient to Him, the Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is** (Al-Baqarah: 116-117). Reference to this has already passed in the commentary on the verses in the first volume of this book.

As for the verse we are currently in, it is directed toward arguing for the negation of the child from the last aspect only—which is that the purpose of its existence is to seek help from it in time of need. That is only conceivable for one who is, by his nature, needy and poor. Allah, the Glorified, is the All-sufficient (الغني) who is not touched by poverty, for He is the owner of whatever is assumed to exist in the heavens and the earth.

His saying: «إِنْ عِندَكُم مِّن سُلْطَانٍ» — **You have no authority**—meaning proof—«بِهَٰذَا» — **for this** is an affirmation that they only said it out of ignorance without evidence. Thus, the sum of the meaning is that you have no evidence for what you said; rather, the evidence is to the contrary, which is that He, the Exalted, is absolutely self-sufficient, and a child is only sought by one who has lack and need. The discourse, according to the terminology of the art of debate, is of the nature of "denial with evidence" (المنع مع السند).

His saying: «أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ» — **Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know?** is a rebuke to them for saying what they have no knowledge of, which is something the human intellect finds repulsive, especially regarding the Lord of the worlds, exalted is His Name.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قُلْ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ لَا يُفْلِحُونَ» — **Say, ‘Indeed those who fabricate lies against Allah will not be felicitous.’** is an intimidation and warning of the evil of the outcome. In the two verses, there is a subtle shift (التفات) that is apparent. Allah first narrated about them in the third person their saying: «اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا» — **‘Allah has taken a son.’** Then He addressed them with the address of one who is displeased and angry at what they attributed to Him and fabricated against Him, saying: «إِنْ عِندَكُم مِّن سُلْطَانٍ بِهَٰذَا ۚ أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ» — **You have no authority for this! Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know?** He addressed them in an indeterminate manner without identifying Himself to them, as He said: «عَلَى اللَّهِ» — **to Allah** and did not say "to Me" or "to Us," to preserve the greatness of His station from mixing with them as one known. Then He turned away from them, transcending the arena of their ignorance, and returned to addressing His Messenger, saying: «قُلْ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ لَا يُفْلِحُونَ» — **Say, ‘Indeed those who fabricate lies against Allah will not be felicitous,’** because it is a warning, and warning is his function.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «مَتَاعٌ فِي الدُّنْيَا ثُمَّ إِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ ثُمَّ نُذِيقُهُمُ الْعَذَابَ الشَّدِيدَ بِمَا كَانُوا يَكْفُرُونَ» — **[It is but] a brief enjoyment in the world, then their return will be to Us, then We shall make them taste the severe punishment because of what they used to disbelieve.** An address to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in which the reason for their lack of success is explained: it is disbelief in Allah, which has nothing corresponding to it except a brief enjoyment in the world, then the return to Allah and the severe punishment they will taste.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Amali* of al-Shaykh: Abu 'Amr informed us, saying: Ahmad informed us, saying: Ya'qub ibn Yusuf ibn Ziyad narrated to us, saying: Nasr ibn Muzahim narrated to us, saying: Muhammad ibn Marwan narrated to us from al-Kalbi from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas regarding: «بِفَضْلِ اللَّهِ وَبِرَحْمَتِهِ» — **In Allah’s grace and His mercy**, he said: "Allah's grace is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and His mercy is Ali (عليه السلام)."

**I say:** It was narrated by al-Tabarsi and Ibn al-Farisi from him in a disconnected (مرسلا) manner, and it was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Khatib and Ibn 'Asakir from him.

In *al-Majma'*: Abu Ja'far al-Baqir (عليه السلام) said: "Allah's grace is the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and His mercy is Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام)."

**I say:** This is because the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is a blessing Allah bestowed upon the worlds through the message and the materials of guidance he brought, and Ali (عليه السلام) is the first to open the door of guardianship (الولاية) and the actualization of the realization of the blessing of guidance; thus he is the mercy. So the report conforms to what we presented in the commentary of the verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Ibn Abbas: «قُلْ بِفَضْلِ اللَّهِ» — **Say, ‘In Allah’s grace’** is the Quran, «وَبِرَحْمَتِهِ» — **and His mercy** is when He made them among the people of the Quran.

**I say:** Meaning, the grace is the materials of gnosis and judgments within it, and the mercy is the actualization of that in those who act upon it. Thus, it returns to what we presented in the commentary of the verse; so observe. There is no contradiction between this report and the previous report in reality.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: «وَمَا تَكُونُ فِي شَأْنٍ» — **You do not engage in any matter** the verse, he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), whenever he recited this verse, would weep intensely."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Majma'* from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام).

In *Amali* of al-Mufid: With his chain from 'Abayah al-Asadi from Ibn Abbas, who said: Commander of the Faithful Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) was asked about His saying, the Exalted: «أَلَا إِنَّ أَوْلِيَاءَ اللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ» — **Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve**. It was said to him: "Who are these friends?" Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) said: "**They are a people who were sincere to Allah in His worship, and they looked at the interior of the world when people looked at its exterior. They recognized its end when it deceived others with its immediate presence. So they left what they knew would leave them, and they put to death from it what they knew would put them to death.**"

Then he said: "**O you who exhaust your soul with the world—running upon its snares—striving in the building of what will be ruined of it! Have you not seen the fallen places of your fathers in the lands, and the fallen places of your sons under the stones and the soil? How many have you nursed with your body and treated with your shroud—seeking doctors for them and crying out to loved ones for them? Yet your help did not avail them, nor did your medicine benefit them.**"

In *Tafsir al-'Ayyashi*: From Marthad al-'Ijli from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), who said: "We found in the book of Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام): «أَلَا إِنَّ أَوْلِيَاءَ اللَّهِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ» — **Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve**..."

He said: "When they perform the obligatory duties (الفرائض) of Allah, adhere to the traditions (السنن) of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, practice God-fearing restraint (التورع) from the prohibitions of Allah, renounce the fleeting splendor of this world, desire that which is with Allah, and earn their sustenance from the wholesome (الطيب) of Allah’s provision—not seeking thereby boastfulness or multiplication of wealth—and then spend from it what is required of them in terms of obligatory rights; those are the ones whom Allah has blessed in what they have earned, and they shall be rewarded for what they have sent forth for their Hereafter."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, al-Hakim, and al-Tirmidhi narrated from ‘Amr ibn al-Jamuh that he heard the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying: "A servant does not realize the truth of pure faith (صريح الإيمان) until he loves for the sake of Allah and hates for the sake of Allah the Exalted. When he loves for Allah and hates for Allah, he has merited guardianship (الولاء) from Allah." *The tradition continues.*

**I say:** These three narrations regarding the meaning of guardianship (الولاية) — *the state of proximity and divine friendship between Allah and His servant* — refer back to one another, and they all align with what we previously established in the commentary on the verse.

In the same source, Ibn al-Mubarak, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Jarir, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding the verse: **ألا إن أولياء الله لا خوف عليهم ولا هم يحزنون** "Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve" (10:62). He said: "Allah is remembered upon seeing them."

**I say:** This should be understood to mean that this is among the effects of their guardianship (الولاية), not that everyone who is like this is necessarily among the people of guardianship, unless it is meant that they are such in all their states and actions. Similar in meaning is what was narrated from Abu al-Duha and Sa‘d from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding the verse; he said: "When they are seen, Allah is remembered."

In the same source, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in *Dhikr al-Mawt*, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu al-Qasim ibn Mandah in *Kitab Su’al al-Qabr* narrated via Abu Ja‘far from Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah, who said: "A man from the desert came to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, inform me about the saying of Allah:

**الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ ـ لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ**
"Those who have faith and are God-conscious (63) for them is good news in the life of this world and in the Hereafter" (10:63-64).'" The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "As for His saying: 'for them is good news in the life of this world,' it is the goodly vision (الرؤيا الحسنة) seen for the believer, by which he is given glad tidings in his worldly life. As for His saying: 'and in the Hereafter,' it is the glad tidings for the believer at the time of death: 'Indeed, Allah has forgiven you and those who carry you to your grave.'"

**I say:** There are many narrations with this meaning from the sources of the *Ahl al-Sunnah*, and al-Saduq narrated it in a detached (مرسلا) form. His saying: "seen for the believer" in the passive voice is more general, whether he sees it himself or someone else sees it for him. His saying: "at the time of death" has been supplemented in some narrations with the glad tidings of Paradise on the Day of Resurrection.

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying: **لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** "for them is good news in the life of this world and in the Hereafter," it is narrated from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding the meaning of the glad tidings in this world: "The righteous vision (الرؤيا الصالحة) which the believer sees for himself or is seen for him; and in the Hereafter, it is Paradise—and it is that which the angels announce to them when they emerge from their graves, and during the Resurrection until they enter Paradise; they are given glad tidings state after state."

**I say:** He said after that: "And this was narrated in a tradition from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله." *End of quote.* A similar report is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام, and al-Qummi narrated it in his *Tafsir* in an unnamed (مضمرا) form.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn Shahr Ashub, from Zurayq, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of the Exalted: **لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** "for them is good news in the life of this world," he said: "It is that they both give him glad tidings of Paradise at the time of death—meaning Muhammad and ‘Ali عليه‌السلام."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Aban ibn ‘Uthman from ‘Uqbah, that he heard Abu ‘Abdullah عليه‌السلام saying: "When a man's soul reaches his chest, he sees." I said: "May I be your ransom, what does he see?" He said: "He sees the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the Messenger of Allah says to him: 'I am the Messenger of Allah, receive glad tidings.' Then he said: 'Then he sees ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه‌السلام, who says: 'I am ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib whom you used to love; indeed, I shall benefit you today.'"

He said: I asked him: "Does any person see this and then return to the world?" He said: "When he sees this, he dies forever." And he considered that a momentous matter. He said: "And that is in the Qur'an in the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى ـ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ ـ لا تَبْدِيلَ لِكَلِماتِ اللهِ** 'Those who have faith and are God-conscious (63) for them is good news in the life of this world and in the Hereafter. There is no altering the words of Allah' (10:63-64)."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated from the Imams of the *Ahl al-Bayt* عليه‌السلام through very numerous paths. His saying: "And he considered that a momentous matter" (وأعظم ذلك) means he regarded it as something great. In the tradition, the saying of the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ** "Those who have faith and are God-conscious" was taken as an independent statement and interpreted accordingly. A similar instance preceded in the narration of *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Jabir ibn ‘Abdullah from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, despite the apparent context (السياق) suggesting that the verse is an explanation for the preceding verse: **أَلا إِنَّ أَوْلِياءَ اللهِ** "Look! The friends of Allah..." This supports what we have previously established in some earlier discussions: that all possible grammatical constructions (التركيبات) in the speech of the Exalted are valid proofs (حجة) that can be invoked, as in His saying: **قُلِ اللهُ ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِي خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ** "Say, ‘Allah.’ Then leave them to play around in their impious gossip" (6:91), and His saying: **قُلِ اللهُ ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِي خَوْضِهِمْ**, and His saying: **قُلِ اللهُ ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ**, and His saying: **قُلِ اللهُ**.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, and al-Tirmidhi—who graded it authentic—and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Indeed, messengership and prophethood have ceased; there is no messenger after me nor any prophet—but there are the heralds (المبشرات).'" They asked: "O Messenger of Allah, what are the heralds?" He said: "The vision (رؤيا) of the Muslim, which is a part of the parts of prophethood."

**I say:** Similar meanings were narrated from Abu Qatadah and ‘Aishah from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, Abu Dawud, and Ibn Majah narrated from Abu Hurayrah, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'When the end of time approaches, the vision of the believer will hardly be false. The most truthful of them in vision are the most truthful of them in speech. The vision of the Muslim is one of forty-six parts of prophethood. Visions are of three types: the righteous vision (الرؤيا الصالحة) is glad tidings from Allah; the vision that causes grief; and the vision resulting from what a man says to himself. If one of you sees what he dislikes, let him stand and spit [lightly], and let him not tell people about it.'" *The tradition continues.*

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from ‘Awf ibn Malik al-Ashja‘i, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Visions are of three types: a terrifying one from Satan to cause the son of Adam grief; a matter he speaks to himself about while awake and then sees in his sleep; and a part of forty-six parts of prophethood.'"

**I say:** As for the division of visions into these three categories, as mentioned in the two narrations—and there are other narrations with this meaning from the sources of the *Ahl al-Sunnah* and others from the Imams of the *Ahl al-Bayt* عليه‌السلام—its clarification will come in the commentary on Surah Yusuf, if Allah the Exalted wills.

As for the righteous vision being one of forty-six parts of prophethood, many narrations have been transmitted regarding this from the sources of the *Ahl al-Sunnah*, narrated from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by a group of companions such as Abu Hurayrah, ‘Ubadah ibn al-Samit, Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, and Abu Razin. Anas, Abu Qatadah, and ‘Aishah also narrated from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that it is among the parts of prophethood, as mentioned previously.

It is narrated from al-Safadi that he explained the narration by stating that the duration of the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله prophethood was twenty-three years, during which he called to his Lord for thirteen years before the Migration (الهجرة) and ten years after it. It has been narrated that revelation (الوحي) used to come to him for six months at the beginning through righteous visions until the Qur'an was revealed. The ratio of six months to twenty-three years is the ratio of one to forty-six.

It has also been narrated from Ibn ‘Umar and Abu Hurayrah from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that it is one of seventy parts of prophethood. If this narration is authentic, the intended meaning of the count would be merely to indicate a large number (التكثير) without specific significance for the number seventy.

Know that "vision" (الرؤيا) is sometimes used in the language of the Qur'an and Hadith to refer to what the seer witnesses which others do not, even if he is not in natural sleep. We have pointed this out in the discussions on prophethood in the second volume of this book. The finest statement in its explanation is his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying: "My eyes sleep, but my heart does not sleep."
